The Y combinator. `Y f = f (Y f)` or `Y = λf.(λx.(f (x x)) λx.(f (x x)))`. Scary because: (1) can be used to demonstrate that untyped lambda calculus is unsound as a deductive system; (2) causes an infinite loop when applied to most functions; (3) single letter variable names.

I'm here for this :heart:

I used the fabulous TekTronix 4014 storage terminal screen back in 1977. It had an incredible 4000 x 3000 pixels, easily 10x what other devices had at the time. To erase the screen you had to press a zap button. The image was rock solid though, as it was etched in slow phosphors, so no flicker at all... It came with two clumsy thumbwheels for XY input, before they had trackballs and mice. Even more astonishing, it had a thermal printer that printed the screen for you in grayscale in a few seconds. Developed for the military, it was such great gear, and Tektronix was and probably still is a great company with excellent products.

I used the fabulous TekTronix 4014 storage terminal screen back in 1977. It had an incredible 4000 x 3000 pixels, easily 10x what other devices had at the time. To erase the screen you had to press a zap button. The image was rock solid though, as it was etched in slow phosphors, so no flicker at all... It came with two clumsy thumbwheels for XY input, before they had trackballs and mice. Even more astonishing, it had a thermal printer that printed the screen for you in grayscale in a few seconds. Developed for the military, it was such great gear, and Tektronix was and probably still is a great company with excellent products.

It is true that the early supporters of computers were the defense dept., banks, and insurance companies. But now with a supercomputer in a billion pockets, we are really only limited by our ability to program. There is however a conspiracy among the tech giants to keep things super complex and under their control, so that no upstart company can crack their monopolies. Complexity is the main weapon used to dominate, and i think you will find many revolutionaries lurking in this slack group, dedicated to toppling the status quo. As <@U6KQ2S410> pointed out the other day, we were promised bicycles for the mind but were given aircraft carriers instead!

Steve Jobs gets a tour of Xerox PARC and is shown Alan Kay's vision of personal dynamic media, embodied by Smalltalk and the Alto (the interrim Dynabook). Jobs takes that vision and gradually perverts it into a system of digital slavery. In Jobs' vision, computers are appliances that are not end user programmable. You can only install Apps, which are only available from the walled garden of the App Store.
I recently mentioned power structures at <https://lists.tildeverse.org/hyperkitty/list/tildeclub@lists.tildeverse.org/message/Z7EBQ2ZCBIQ7YMA7Q3RUJWWB4LBIFS3M/> So :heart:

Hi Ed! I'd love to talk flexibility with you. Here's a sample of my thoughts:

Software is the most malleable material in existence: it can do / be / simulate almost anything. But the current way to do so (i.e. programming) is incredibly locked down and prescriptive. This is bizarre because the tools for making software is ... other software!

My solution is ... something like this (I can explain more later if it piques your interest):

<https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/b/ba/DrawingHands.jpg>

Superhuman is not the only product doing this. There are dozens of tools, like Yesware, that track such data. I’m not saying or validating Superhuman, but this concern is not exclusive to them. There are deeper concerns, but nonetheless, it’s all about trust. Even using Gmail is a decision based on trust, yet Google is under a lot of scrutiny for their data collection. Pick your battles :wink:

software is malleable like sand is malleable. Software has a very tiny cell size, like sand, and as such is very tedious to construct anything. It is not really a flexible material, it is merely a microscopic rigid material, and has to be used in great abundance to achieve good things. The fact that we are not in an era of interchangeable parts means that people have to build their castles over and over custom made, instead of using pre-fab small pieces like lego. Lego is arguably the greatest toy ever invented, founded on the 8 bump brick which snaps together in many ways, but always perfectly. We need better data interchange between pieces and then it will be a much more flexible medium. It's not locked down really; none of the pieces snap together that well.
Tbh I hadn't heard this was a feature until you mentioned it. I wonder whether this is something which you can toggle on/off?

In software, the shovels and buckets are also made of sand, and you can sculpt yourself an excavator (and the things made from sand can be used without crumbling apart)

But yes, as soon as you try to alter anything made of sand, it can crumble. So maybe we need sand-bricks that are easy to reassemble without having to think about the sand they're made of?

Anyway, nice analogy. Software is super powerful and expressive, but we need to find ways to make it less like working with sand. The tricky part is that we've got to use sand (at least initially) to shape the sand into something less sandy.

I've never used it, but I follow the company because I'm fascinated by the whole idea of a $30 a month email client. Another interesting bit is that every user goes through a 30 minute onboarding session before they can start using it <https://medium.com/toyboxsystems/onboard-your-users-lesson-learned-from-superhuman-6db2b73042c2>

Their onboarding makes sure you love it. It’s a brilliant idea because you get immediate feedback and make sure the user understands all the features, setups up properly, and has the highest chance of success.

An interesting question Superhuman brings up is what other apps become viable if you can charge $360/year per user? E.g., if Superhuman is a "high-end email client", could you do the same thing with a high-end todo list, or a high-end word processor? And if not, what's unique about email that this approach works for it and not other categories?
it’s not just whoever showed up first—most programmers were originally women, for example
I mean narrowly whoever showed up first to any given product or language or API or community.

it’s definitely a huge contributing factor—especially since people with resources and support and a safety net are more likely able to tinker with new things. was just pointing out that there are a myriad of structural factors beyond just first-past-the-post

Yeah, I didn't intend for it to be comprehensive.

But can you give examples of some other factors? I'm looking to identify my blind spots.

i meant the ones that I at least would consider obvious—eg: tons of computing, especially in poorer countries, happens on mobile. and those systems aren’t very friendly to open source unless you also own a laptop
another thing is like community/discrimination keeping people out or forcing them out after a while

another thing is just straight up state violence: turing was in the middle of inventing a really interesting take on chaos theory before anyone knew it as chaos theory before the british government murdered him

I interpret "whoever happened to show up first" not as who did the first acts of programming / computer science (in many cases, women), but rather as whatever power structures were imposed first are what dominated. I'm thinking ethea like top-down militarism, capitalism, libertarianism, anarchism.

It's easy to imagine how different computing would be if it were entirely relegated to artists as a medium for creative expression, and were never adopted (some might say co-opted) by business and the military. Or were it initially seen as a polytechnic trade or craft, rather than an avenue to research mathematics and physics. Imagine if the computational substrate were emergent from nature, like leather or fabric, rather than emergent from mechanics and electronics — how that would change the dissemination of knowledge and practice in the early days. (I don't mean emergent from "fiber arts" — I know about card weaving and there's a loom in my house. I mean emergent from fiber itself.)

hm—we’re definitely on the same page here—but I think there usually are societies and power structures pre-existing before capitalism/militarism/whatever comes in and takes over—I guess that’s my only point

Adobe Creative suite has a similar pricing model for example and so does Figma, Unity3D, office 365, Maya LT, etc.  Superhuman is interesting because it's an email client, and most consumers will want their email client for free. There are a lot of people whose primary tool is email, and would find a professional email client valuable. I think the market for people that would pay $30 a month for a very good email client is larger than most professional tools

text: <@UJKKBAMLL> Then that's dozens of tools that are all sketchy and I wouldn't knowingly use any of them. This isn't a battle, it's just hygiene. Most people don't know the extent to which tools like Superhuman are disrespectful of norms, so it bears mentioning when it comes up.

<@UBSMEUXAA> Regarding professional tools, I'd say those prices are more for specialized tools, e.g., the number of people who use Maya or the Adobe Suite is far less than say an Office Suite. Supply and demand dictates that apps with less users are always more expensive. (You listed MS Office in your group, above but that's only $70/year for several apps, so that's more inline with traditional software pricing for non-specialized software.) The reason Superhuman is unique is that it's charging specialized prices for a tool that *everyone* uses.

I think this is really more bizarre than people realize. Historically an app would want as many users as possible, and power and value correlate with the number of users, because it means having more resources to develop your software more quickly than competitors. Superhuman subverts this whole script, I can't think of a single example of another app that works this way? E.g., tries to create higher quality by deliberately having less (but higher paying) users than competitors.
on the business model front it’s actually a matter of segmenting the market—a chef’s knife is something everyone who cooks uses, but someone who cooks for eight hours a day will pay a lot more

Yeah I agree 100% with the chefs knife analogy! The case I’m making is that that’s new to the software market, I can’t think of any example besides Superhuman that tries to be the chefs knife of its category. E.G., I’d say things like Maya and the Adobe Suite are more like table saws.

A chefs knife is more expensive because it’s more expensive to manufacture, software doesn’t have manufacturing costs. It has r&d costs, but traditionally the r&d cost isn’t directly passed on to the consumer. E.G., some of the most expensive software to develop is some of this cheapest to use.

The cost of software traditionally is something pretty simple like r&d cost divided by market size. Superhuman is something that could be used by almost anyone, but it’s priced like a specialized tool.
i see what you’re saying now! yeah i’m gonna be on the lookout
i mean there aren’t that many open protocols that everyone uses
it suggests that there might be like researchers who’d pay for a heavy duty web browser
i can only think of hardware examples like headphones for producers and musicians/monitors for video editors

i think you’re on to something here—is email the only thing universal enough to support this kind of business model? closest software analogs might be paying for plugins and extensions to eg wordpress or excel (because they’re both the platform/protocol and the product it’s hard to sustain an alternative outside the primary apps)
Web browser feels like a great candidate to get the Superhuman-treatment. Especially with the trend of power user features disappearing to make them more secure.

<@UE0ETTCG7> Thinking about (sorry, all iOS apps, but that’s my home turf) <https://www.omnigroup.com/>, <https://culturedcode.com/things/>, <https://ulysses.app/>, to name just a few — how would you say their business models are different from “trying to be the chef’s knives of their category”?

text: All of these analogies only work if we ignore free (as in beer) software because free software works differently enough that it ends up warping prices.  If you remove free software from the conversation, than the apps you listed are actually priced dirt cheap. For example, Ulysses is $40 a year, compared to Superhuman's $360. Ulysses/Things/Omnifocus for the most part are priced the same as most mass-market, unspecialized, paid software, e.g., similar to MS Office ($70/year). Superhuman is priced closer to the first tier of more specialized expensive software, e.g., something like the Adobe Suite ($600/year), or Ableton Live ($1000+/one time fee plus upgrades $500 upgrades every three years or so).
<@UE0ETTCG7> So what’s the magic number then where it transitions from one into the other business model? Or am I missing something in your argument that it’s not just about the price?
Great question, I don't really think about it in terms of specific numbers, more like on a graph where would the groupings be? E.g., 360 would be closer to 600 than to 40.
I guess if forced to pick a number I'd probably say $200/year?

Adobe charges $21/mo. per app for single apps, so that's probably a good lower bounds, since Adobe is probably the closest to mass-market of the more specialized apps. Although that's probably a bit high because Adobe wants to entice you into the platform lock-in of signing up for the full suite. (And that's outside of Adobe's photo plan, which only exists because so many platforms offer free photo tools, warping the market.)

<@UE0ETTCG7> Interesting. I didn’t expect you to actually come up with a number. I think I see what you mean with the graph example. 

However, doesn’t that make it a very subjective metric? And I have a similar feeling about where general ends and specialized begins…

I do agree that Superhuman is an interesting phenomenon to watch as they are clearly pushing several boundaries (and not all in a good way). I guess I just don’t find them special in the same way that you do. :-)

The easy objective comparison here is just  comparing the price of Superhuman to other email clients, e.g., the easiest competitor I can think of is Airmail which is just $10 a year... the fact that Superhuman is charge 36 times their competitors seems pretty clear evidence they’re doing something radically different?

Everyone uses email but many people don't use it as a professional tool or their primary professional tool. There are a lot of free or cheap photo editors that are widely used, but professionals still pay for Adobe CC.  My point was I don't think Superhuman is very different from subscription tools you described as more niche, with the exception that I do think it has a larger addresseable market.

I agree 100% with this statement "Everyone uses email but many people don't use it as a professional tool or their primary professional tool." Marketing Superhuman in that way (and then charging way more for it) is a fair summary of what I'd call new about what they are doing. I also agree photo editing is in the process of being disrupted these days, and that's also an interesting phenomena, but that's the inverse of Superhuman, there a more specialized skill is in the process of becoming democratized, e.g., Apple folded their tool for professional photographers, Aperture, into their built-in photo tool for everyone, Photos.
I obviously forgot to mention how terrible I am at keeping up with Slack messages. :slightly_smiling_face: Glad to have triggered a bit of interesting discussion…

On power: I’m hearing people describe how a variety of power differences at multiple scales of organization have affected the history of what the world of computing looks like today and who has agency over computing.

<@UC2A2ARPT> re: artists, that’s an interesting thought experiment. I haven’t played with them much, but from what I’ve seen programming tools built by/for artists are some of the most inspiring “future of coding” projects, and I’ve often wished I had things like that for scientific work, which I see as actually not that different from artistic work in terms of process.
E.g., visual manipulation of dataflow graphs for computer music (Max/MSP/Pd), and for visual effects (as <@U6FKVSVCK> was talking about at the SF meetup on Thursday).

(To be fair, there are attempts at tools like that for scientific workflows and general-purpose computing. Why weren’t they effective? I suspect that—as has probably been pointed out a lot here—doing something like this well is quite difficult, and that the people doing them generally did not have the incentives, resources and/or UX mindset to get there.)

<@UCGAK10LS> on Datalog: our core code is in Rust, so a while back we played with Mentat (<https://github.com/mozilla/mentat>), which is now dead (and did not yet have a real rules engine). We’re now working on our own thing built on SQLite, which now exists as a hacky Python prototype and approximately looks like: some tables for ground facts (input relations); rules that get materialized into other tables; and a simple query API that (hackily) translates predicates into simple SQL.
Does laser etching work on a pumpkin?

Laser cutters don't work well because the pumpkin doesn't have a flat surface, and the laser is only focussed over a short interval. Also, the entire pumpkin needs to be inside the machine for laser safety. But CNC mills work just fine. <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3l8LYZPv44I>

text: in case you don't have the time, my takeaway from this was that they counterintuitively have decided to _shrink_ their market by various means (pricing, invites, screening) so that they can hyperfocus their product effort on delighting and expanding the group of people who feel like they can't live without their product

i personally suspect that it's quite possible their pricing will change if they feel like they've ended up building a mass market product, but they don't feel like they're at the stage where they actually _want_ a huge user base. whether that's brilliant or arrogant is TBD
Haven’t watched the video yet, but the way you describe it reminds me of Elon Musk’s masterplan strategy for Tesla and SpaceX. See <https://www.tesla.com/blog/master-plan-part-deux>

attachments.text: */ The first master plan that I wrote 10 years ago is now in the final stages of completion. It wasn't all that complicated and basically consisted of:

text: Now I have watched the video, and wow… they weaponized analytics in a dangerously clever way. How they disregard their users who aren’t excited about their product and double down on those who are somewhat dependent on it reminds me of Dominic Cummings’ strategy in the UK’s Leave campaign and Cambridge Analytica — it’s undeniably effective and also leaves a bad aftertaste…

To be fair: the strategy is sound and I would encourage people to use this to get to product-market fit.
Just don’t leave behind your ethical standards for success.

on some level, reminiscent of Facebook's approach of starting at top-tier colleges and only expanding once potential new colleges hit critical mass. except that instead of doing this make sure the social network was densely represented enough to be sticky, Superhuman is doing it for driving their iterative product development. but for sure both examples also have a healthy dose of elitism and FOMO at play.
i think it’s really just a tactical application of the approach in <http://www.paulgraham.com/startupideas.html>
if there aren’t some people who like your product enough to pay for your product, your business is going to die

Power is one of the key problems with software. It’s not just a problem with the iThings and the appstore. It’s also a problem perennially present in Smalltalk where any code can do anything, and it’s more subtly present in the teamwork dynamics of software development. What developers would you trust? What software libraries and applications do you trust?  The people that write the code (especially in languages like smalltalk and in compilers) are the people that “twiddle” the DNA of what we’re allowed to do… ?

elements.text: Power is one of the key problems with software. It’s not just a problem with the iThings and the appstore. It’s also a problem perennially present in Smalltalk where any code can do anything, and it’s more subtly present in the teamwork dynamics of software development. What developers would you trust? What software libraries and applications do you trust?  The people that write the code (especially in languages like smalltalk and in compilers) are the people that “twiddle” the DNA of what we’re allowed to do… ?

text: In one bank, the lead dev pretty much didn’t trust us to make decisions - he wanted to monopolise them all. We ended up having secret meetings in the building to be able to talk openly about how to build things. That’s just an extreme example of the problems associated with “power” in software. Scaling software may be at least partly difficult because of the need to handle trust/power at scale.
